text: The surgeon begins the case with lysis of adhesions, addressing dense adhesions across the midline and lower abdomen, a process that visually involves careful separation of adhered tissues.

After thirty to forty minutes of dissection, the surgeon exposes the rim of the secondary hernia within the primary hernia. The adhesions at this stage are visibly less dense and are easily dissected.
The surgeon carefully dissects the incarcerated colon away from the hernia sac, visibly pulling on an epithelial appendage to reduce the colon.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the first hernia, focusing on the peristomal site, which also presents as a hernia. The dissection extends to the lateral sidewall, revealing areas of adhesion.
The surgeon removes all adhesions, ensuring there are no remaining sources of obstruction within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins retro rectus dissection on the patient's left side, progressing towards the hernia site after removing all adhesions.

The surgeon carefully preserves the posterior tissue while dissecting medially to the ostomy site hernia, ensuring the visceral sac remains without tension. This approach allows for additional centimeters of tissue and sac preservation.

The surgeon continues the dissection into the retromuscular space, progressing towards the retropubic area due to the complexity and size of the hernia. Preparations are made to initiate the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.
The surgeon approaches the medial aspect of the rectus muscle near the peristomal site, preparing to initiate the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the neurovascular bundles and the semilunar line, preparing to perform a transversus abdominis release (TAR) while avoiding challenges in the retro-rectus space near the ostomy site.

The surgeon performs a left-sided Transversus Abdominis Release (TAR) by identifying and incising the posterior lamella, followed by taking muscle fibers. As the dissection progresses cephalad, the surgeon moves medially near the preperitoneal fat to simplify the procedure.
The surgeon releases the muscle and begins dissection towards the prior ostomy site, focusing on the retromuscular space.
The surgeon demonstrates the recruitment of a portion of the hernia sac at the ostomy site, highlighting the visible progress in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection, obtaining additional tissue to minimize potential posterior tension in the surgical area.

The surgeon integrates the anterior fascia and hernia sac at the prior ostomy site to eliminate dead space, followed by placating a large inception hernia. The visible hernia is only a small part of a larger defect, and the surgeon avoids integrating the linea alba into the closure to prevent seroma formation.
The surgeon works to eliminate dead space in the surgical area, preparing for optimal tissue mesh interface to minimize seroma formation. The team then redocks onto the patient's left side.

The surgeon performs contralateral retromuscular dissection, followed by posterior defect closure and mesh placement. The dissection on this side proceeds smoothly, allowing for unification of the tissue plane.

The surgeon observes the rolled-up rectus muscles on one side and achieves medialization by releasing the posterior sheath. The rectus muscles are visibly rolled up, and a standard retro rectus dissection is performed leading to a transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The surgeon performs a dissection through the posterior lamella on this side, carefully protecting the neurovascular bundles. The dissection continues through the muscle down to the tendinous portion of the transversus abdominis (TA).

The surgeon identifies small fenestrations related to prior eight-millimeter trocar placements, which will be closed. A loose visceral sac is visible, allowing for easy closure without tension on the posterior structures.
The surgeon proceeds with defect closure, having extended the dissection into the retropubic space. A large hernia is visible despite previous placation efforts.

The surgeon re-approximates the muscle layers and proceeds with the placement of a 30x30 mesh into the prepared space. A tissue spray is applied for additional fixation to the posterior, and a drain is placed. The mesh is rolled tightly to fit through the eight-millimeter trocars, following techniques learned from international collaboration.
